worth_it_explanation: IAS measures viewability, invalid traffic (IVT), and brand safety on impressions it can actually reach. When the measured rate, the share of total impressions IAS covers, is low or unknown, the Signal reports reflect only a slice of the buy. The unmeasured impressions carry no viewability data, no IVT filtration signal, and no brand-safety classification. These checks require only IAS Signal and a comparison against the DSP delivery report. Confirming the measured rate, understanding what drives gaps in it, and reconciling the measured impression count against the billed count takes one review session and gives the team a clear picture of where the data is trustworthy.
intro:
Integral Ad Science (IAS) measurement coverage describes the share of an advertiser's total impressions that IAS actually measures for viewability, invalid traffic filtration, and brand safety. IAS measures an impression by attaching a JavaScript tag to the creative or by receiving server-to-server data from a demand-side platform (DSP) integration. An impression that IAS cannot reach through either path is counted as unmeasured. It does not appear in Signal with a flag; it simply does not appear at all.

Coverage gaps arise from several sources. Some environments, including certain mobile in-app placements and connected television (CTV) inventory, may require the Open Measurement SDK (OM SDK) or a server-side integration that is not present in every publisher's stack. Campaigns running without an IAS tag attached to the creative, or with a DSP integration that has lapsed or was never configured for that specific campaign, also produce unmeasured impressions. A measured rate that is low, declining, or simply unknown signals that a meaningful portion of the buy operates without quality data.

These checks confirm that the team knows the measured rate, investigates the causes when it falls short, and reconciles the impression volume IAS reports against the billed impression count from the DSP or ad server so coverage gaps do not go undetected.

audit_checks.title: The IAS measured rate is not tracked, so the team does not know what share of spend has quality data
audit_checks.impact_rationale: When no one tracks the measured rate, viewability, IVT, and brand-safety data from IAS Signal may represent a fraction of the total buy without any indication of how large that fraction is. A practitioner reading Signal cannot distinguish between a healthy account with full coverage and one where half the impressions are invisible to IAS. Decisions about creative quality, publisher performance, and fraud risk are made against an incomplete data set without the team realizing it.

educate.base:
Integral Ad Science (IAS) Signal reports viewability, IVT, and brand-safety data for the impressions it measures. It does not automatically surface the share of impressions it did not measure. A practitioner who reads Signal without knowing the measured rate is reading a sample of the buy without knowing the sample size.

The measured rate is the ratio of impressions IAS measured to the total impressions the campaign served. An account with a high measured rate, approaching the total billed impression count, produces Signal data that represents the full buy. An account with a low measured rate produces Signal data that may accurately describe only the measured portion, which could be concentrated in environments or placements that happen to support IAS tagging, while the unmeasured portion sits in environments that do not.

IAS Signal includes a measured impression count in its reporting. The team can compare this count against the total impression count from the DSP or ad server for the same date range to derive the measured rate. This comparison is not automated in Signal; it requires pulling numbers from two sources and dividing them. Without that step, the measured rate is unknown and the Signal data has no denominator.

A few signs this check applies:
  • The team reviews IAS Signal reports regularly but no one can state the measured rate for the account or for any specific campaign
  • There is no documented baseline for what the measured rate was at the start of a campaign, so changes in coverage go unnoticed
  • Signal reports are used to evaluate publisher viewability or IVT performance without any check on whether those publishers are fully covered by IAS measurement
You may want to pull the IAS measured impression count from Signal and compare it to the total impression count from the DSP for the same period before reading any Signal data as representative of the full buy.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a retail advertiser running campaigns across display, video, and CTV, the measured rate may differ significantly by environment. CTV inventory may require an OM SDK integration that not all publishers have implemented, so the CTV measured rate may be lower than the display rate. You may want to confirm the measured rate by format before drawing conclusions from the viewability or IVT data in Signal.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead-generation advertiser running programmatic display and video, the measured rate tells you how much of the IVT filtration and viewability data reflects the actual buy. You may want to confirm the measured rate for each campaign before using Signal data to evaluate publisher quality or justify blocking decisions.

educate.learn_more_note: Detailed guidance on the measured rate metric in IAS Signal, including how IAS calculates coverage across tag-based and server-side integrations, is available in the IAS advertiser help center. Access requires an active IAS account login through the IAS Signal platform (helpcenter.integralplatform.com). Your IAS account team can provide a measured-rate breakdown by campaign and environment as part of an account review.

investigate.intro: Pull the measured impression count from IAS Signal and compare it against the total impression count from the DSP for the same date range.

ias_signal.label: IAS Signal
steps: Log in to IAS Signal and open the reporting view for the advertiser account. Set the date range to match the period you want to evaluate.
Find the measured impression count in the Signal report. This column may be labeled 'Measured Impressions' or 'Measurable Impressions' depending on the report type. Note the total for the period.
Note any breakdown by format or environment if the report allows it. Display, video, and CTV may show meaningfully different measured counts.
Export or record the measured impression total for comparison in the next step.

dsp_or_ad_server.label: DSP or ad server
steps: Open the DSP (demand-side platform) or ad server for the same advertiser and pull a delivery report covering the same date range you used in IAS Signal.
Note the total impression count from the DSP. This is the denominator for the measured rate calculation.

Divide the IAS measured impression count by the DSP total impression count to get the measured rate. For example, 8 million measured impressions out of 10 million billed impressions is an 80 percent measured rate.
If the measured rate is below 80 percent, note the gap in impressions and proceed to the next check to investigate the likely causes.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records the IAS measured impression count, the DSP total impression count, and the resulting measured rate. An account where the team can state the current measured rate and compares it against a documented baseline passes the monitoring check, regardless of the rate itself. An account where no one can state the measured rate, or where no baseline exists to compare against, is a fail, recorded with the IAS measured count, the DSP total count, and the calculated rate.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the IAS measured impression count and the DSP total impression count for the same date range above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that the IAS measured rate for {{site_name}} is not tracked, so the team cannot state what share of the buy IAS actually measured for viewability, IVT, and brand safety during the review period. IAS Signal reports viewability and IVT data only for the impressions it reached; without knowing the measured rate, those figures have no reliable denominator. {{voice_recommend}} pulling the measured impression count from IAS Signal and comparing it against the total impression count from the DSP for the same period to establish the current measured rate, documenting that rate as a baseline for future campaigns, and reviewing it at a consistent cadence so changes in coverage are noticed while a campaign is still running.

audit_checks.title: A low or declining measured rate is not investigated against its likely causes
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A low measured rate reduces the reliability of every Signal metric without indicating why coverage is short. Tag gaps on creative, inactive DSP integrations, and environments IAS cannot measure through a JavaScript tag each produce the same symptom in different ways. Without tracing the gap to its cause, the team cannot determine whether the fix is a trafficking change, an integration update, or an acknowledgment that certain inventory will always be outside IAS coverage.

educate.base:
A measured rate below expectations can trace to several distinct causes, and the correct response differs depending on which one applies. Understanding which cause is driving the gap requires looking at the buy from multiple angles rather than treating a low measured rate as a single undifferentiated problem.

The first cause is a tag gap. If some creatives were trafficked without an IAS tag attached, the impressions those creatives serve are unmeasured. This is a trafficking error and can be corrected by adding the IAS tag to the affected creatives, though doing so mid-flight only addresses future impressions.

The second cause is an inactive or misconfigured DSP integration. IAS can receive impression data directly from a DSP through a server-to-server connection rather than through a JavaScript tag on the creative. If that integration is inactive, disconnected, or configured for only some campaigns, the impressions those campaigns serve do not reach Signal. Confirming the integration status in both the DSP and IAS Signal identifies whether this path is contributing to the gap.

The third cause is an environment IAS cannot measure through a standard tag. Some mobile in-app placements and connected television (CTV) environments do not execute JavaScript, which means a creative-level IAS tag will not fire. For these environments, measurement depends on the publisher or the supply-side platform (SSP) having implemented the Open Measurement SDK (OM SDK), an industry standard maintained by the IAB Tech Lab that enables third-party viewability and IVT measurement in non-browser environments. Where OM SDK is not present, those impressions may fall outside IAS coverage by design rather than by error.

A few signs this check applies:
  • The measured rate is below 70 to 80 percent of the billed impression count and no investigation has been documented
  • The buy includes CTV or mobile in-app inventory that could not be covered by a JavaScript tag, but no one has confirmed whether those publishers support OM SDK
  • The measured rate declined between two consecutive reporting periods without any change in the media plan that would explain the drop
You may want to break the measured impression count down by format and publisher in IAS Signal before attributing the gap to any single cause. Different causes tend to cluster in different parts of the buy.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a retail advertiser with CTV spend, the CTV measured rate is the most likely source of a gap because JavaScript-based IAS tags do not fire in most CTV environments. You may want to confirm with the CTV publishers in the buy whether they have implemented OM SDK. Where OM SDK is not present, the CTV impressions will remain outside IAS coverage until the publisher adds it.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead-generation advertiser buying heavily in mobile in-app environments, some in-app placements may not execute the JavaScript IAS tag reliably. You may want to check whether the DSP integration is active for those placements as an alternative measurement path. Server-side delivery data does not depend on the creative environment executing JavaScript.

educate.learn_more_note: IAS publishes guidance on which environments are measurable through tag-based versus server-side integration paths, and what the expected measured-rate ranges are by format and environment. This guidance is available in the IAS advertiser help center, which requires an active IAS account login through the IAS Signal platform (helpcenter.integralplatform.com). Your IAS account team can also provide an environment-by-environment coverage analysis as part of a measurement review.

investigate.intro: Break down the measured impression count in IAS Signal by format or environment, then check the DSP integration status and creative trafficking to identify where the gap originates.

ias_signal.label: IAS Signal
steps: Log in to IAS Signal and open the reporting view. Apply a breakdown by environment or format if the report supports it (display, video, mobile in-app, CTV). Note which environments show lower measured counts relative to the total.

Check the DSP integration settings inside IAS Signal. Look for a section listing active DSP or partner connections. For each integration listed, confirm the status shows as active and that it covers the campaigns in scope.
If any DSP integration shows as inactive or covers only specific campaigns, note the campaigns it excludes. Those campaigns' impressions would arrive in Signal only if the creatives carry an IAS tag.

dsp_or_ad_server.label: DSP or ad server
steps: In the DSP, pull a delivery report broken down by format or environment for the same period. Compare the impression volume by environment against the IAS Signal measured counts for the same breakdown.

Identify environments where the DSP shows high impression volume but Signal shows a disproportionately low measured count. This signals either a tag gap or a measurement limitation in that environment.

Check the creative trafficking for campaigns where the measured rate is lowest. Confirm whether an IAS tag is attached to every active creative. If some creatives lack the tag, note the campaign name and the creative IDs affected.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records the measured rate by environment (where the report allows), the DSP integration status, and whether IAS tags are attached to all active creatives. An account where the low measured rate has been traced to a documented cause (tag gap, inactive integration, or an environment without OM SDK) and where the remediable causes have been addressed passes. An account where the measured rate is below expectations and no cause has been investigated or documented is a fail, recorded with the measured rate, the environments showing the largest gap, and any integration or tagging issues found.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the measured rate by environment, the DSP integration status, and any creative tagging gaps found above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}}'s IAS measured rate is below expectations and the gap has not been traced to its cause. A low measured rate means that viewability, IVT, and brand-safety data in Signal reflects only a portion of the buy, and the size and composition of the unmeasured share is unknown. {{voice_recommend}} breaking down the measured impression count by environment in IAS Signal to identify where the gap is concentrated, confirming that the DSP integration is active and covers all campaigns in scope, verifying that every active creative carries an IAS tag, and, for CTV and mobile in-app placements that cannot execute JavaScript tags, confirming with the publishers whether Open Measurement SDK (OM SDK) is implemented as the measurement path.

audit_checks.title: IAS measured impression count is not reconciled against the billed impression count from the DSP
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Without reconciling the IAS measured impression count against the billed count from the DSP, the team cannot confirm how much of the buy has quality data. A coverage gap that grows over a flight period when a DSP integration lapses or a tag is removed mid-campaign produces no error in Signal. The only way to detect it is a routine comparison of the two impression counts.

educate.base:
IAS Signal reports a measured impression count: the number of impressions IAS received data for, either through a JavaScript tag firing or through a DSP server-to-server integration delivering impression data. The DSP or ad server reports a separate billed impression count: the number of impressions the DSP delivered and charged against the budget. These two numbers come from different sources and use different counting methods, so they will not match exactly. The Media Rating Council (MRC) publishes impression measurement guidelines that address expected variance between measurement systems; some discrepancy between a verification vendor's count and a DSP's count is normal and reflects methodological differences such as when in the ad delivery chain an impression is recorded.

What the reconciliation is looking for is not an exact match but a coverage signal: is the IAS measured count close to the DSP billed count, or is there a gap large enough to indicate that a meaningful share of impressions is outside IAS coverage? A measured count that is consistently 10 to 15 percent below the billed count may reflect normal methodological variance. A measured count that is 40 percent below the billed count, or one that was close at campaign launch and has fallen progressively further behind over the flight, points to a coverage problem worth investigating.

Reconciliation is a manual step because it requires pulling numbers from two separate platforms. Without a documented cadence for doing it, a coverage gap that develops mid-flight, such as a DSP integration that disconnects, can go unnoticed until the post-campaign report reveals data that does not reflect the full buy. The MRC's impression measurement guidelines provide the industry framework for understanding expected discrepancy ranges between measurement systems.

A few signs this check applies:
  • Post-campaign reports are reviewed in Signal only, with no comparison against the DSP delivery report for the same period
  • The team knows the viewability and IVT rates from Signal but cannot state the measured rate or the size of the gap between the IAS count and the DSP count
  • There is no documented in-flight review process that includes a comparison of IAS and DSP impression counts
You may want to establish a regular cadence for pulling the IAS measured count and the DSP billed count side by side so a coverage gap that develops mid-flight is caught while the campaign is still running.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a retail advertiser running a high-volume display and video campaign, a 40 percent measurement gap means roughly 40 percent of the impressions served carry no viewability, IVT, or brand-safety data. You may want to confirm the IAS measured count against the DSP delivery count at least once per week during an active campaign so a lapsed integration does not leave a large portion of a seasonal flight unprotected.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead-generation advertiser where publisher quality directly affects lead volume, the IAS measured count tells you how much of the buy the IVT and viewability data covers. You may want to pull the reconciliation at least once mid-flight so any coverage drop caused by a trafficking or integration change is corrected before the campaign ends.

educate.learn_more_note: IAS guidance on expected discrepancy ranges between IAS Signal impression counts and DSP delivery counts by environment and integration type is available in the IAS advertiser help center. Access requires an active IAS account login through the IAS Signal platform (helpcenter.integralplatform.com). Your IAS account team can walk through the reconciliation methodology and advise on what discrepancy levels are within normal range for your specific media mix.

investigate.intro: Pull the IAS measured impression count from Signal and the total billed impression count from the DSP for the same date range, then compare them to derive the measured rate and note any trend over the campaign flight.

ias_signal.label: IAS Signal
steps: Log in to IAS Signal and open the reporting view for the advertiser account. Set the date range to the full campaign flight or the most recent available period.

Pull the total measured impression count from the report. If the report supports a weekly or daily breakdown, pull that too so you can identify whether the measured count has been stable or declining over the flight.
Note the measured impression count and the date range for comparison.

dsp_or_ad_server.label: DSP or ad server
steps: Open the DSP or ad server for the same advertiser and pull a delivery report covering the identical date range used in IAS Signal.
Note the total billed impression count from the DSP. This is the denominator.

Divide the IAS measured impression count by the DSP billed count to calculate the measured rate. If you pulled a daily or weekly breakdown from IAS, compare the trend to the DSP delivery trend to see whether the gap has been growing, stable, or improving.

If the measured rate has declined from one period to the next without a corresponding change in the media plan, note the date the decline started. That date is a useful starting point for checking whether a tag was removed or a DSP integration went inactive.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records the IAS measured impression count, the DSP billed impression count, the resulting measured rate, and any trend over the campaign flight. An account with a documented reconciliation cadence and a stable measured rate passes. An account where the reconciliation has never been performed, or where the measured rate has declined during the flight without any noted cause, is a fail, recorded with the measured count, the billed count, the calculated rate, and the date range covered.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the IAS measured impression count, the DSP billed impression count, and the date range covered above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}}'s IAS measured impression count is not reconciled against the billed impression count from the DSP, so the team cannot confirm what share of the buy has IAS viewability, IVT, and brand-safety data. Without this comparison, a coverage gap that develops mid-flight when a DSP integration disconnects or a tag is removed from active creatives produces no visible error in Signal and may not be detected until after the campaign ends. {{voice_recommend}} pulling the IAS measured count and the DSP billed count side by side for the same date range at a consistent cadence during each flight, documenting the measured rate at campaign launch as a baseline, and investigating any period where the rate has declined relative to that baseline.
